content.text: Prompt chaining decomposes a complex task into a fixed sequence of LLM calls where each stage receives the previous stage's output as structured context. Rather than attempting everything in a single mega-prompt, the work is broken into focused, independently-tunable steps — extract, classify, generate, review — and each step is evaluated before proceeding to the next. This deterministic pipeline is the simplest reliable multi-stage AI pattern, and it underpins most production agentic systems before they add autonomy.
A sequential prompt chain is a
of LLM calls where the output of step
is injected into the prompt of step

. Each stage has its own system prompt, its own model choice, its own temperature setting, and its own output schema. The calling code — not the model — decides what happens next. There is no model-chosen branching, no autonomous tool selection, and no memory loop. Control flow is explicit Python or similar orchestration code.
(produce a final response conditioned on both). A fourth stage is commonly added:
— a cheap verification call that checks whether the generated output meets quality criteria before it is returned or persisted.
Prompt chaining is sometimes called a

(distinct from chain-of-thought, which is a reasoning technique inside a single call). Every stage runs to completion and returns a result before the next stage begins, making it synchronous and easy to instrument.

A single monolithic prompt trying to extract entities, classify intent, enforce business rules, and generate a polished response is brittle. The model faces conflicting sub-tasks simultaneously, error recovery is difficult, and different sub-tasks need different temperature and sampling settings. A typo in the extraction logic silently corrupts the final answer because there is no intermediate checkpoint.
Sequential chaining solves this by making each intermediate result

. Teams can evaluate stage-1 extraction in isolation using a golden test set, fix regressions without touching stage-3 generation, and use a cheap fast model (Haiku / GPT-4o-mini) for classification while reserving a powerful model (Sonnet / GPT-4) for the creative generation stage. The result is a system with dramatically better debuggability and lower per-call cost than a single heavy prompt.
In production, prompt chains also enable

: if the classification stage determines a query falls outside the system's scope, the chain aborts before paying for the expensive generation call. This is a significant cost and latency win for high-traffic systems.
: each stage has a single, clearly defined responsibility, making prompts shorter, more precise, and easier to iterate.
: use a fast, cheap model (temperature 0) for deterministic classification stages and a larger, creative model for generation stages, cutting cost without sacrificing quality.
: intermediate outputs can be validated, logged, cached, and replayed — enabling offline evaluation against golden sets and exact reproduction of any chain execution.
: a gate stage can short-circuit the pipeline before expensive downstream calls when input quality or confidence is insufficient.
: unlike autonomous agent loops, the chain's execution graph is known at design time — the same stages run in the same order every time, making latency and cost predictable.
: CI pipelines can evaluate each stage independently with a dedicated eval set, catching regressions at the stage level rather than only at the end-to-end level.
: grounding the generation stage with structured facts extracted in stage 1 significantly reduces confabulation compared to asking a single call to do everything.
: a structured error in stage 1 (wrong JSON schema, hallucinated field) silently corrupts every downstream stage. Explicit output validation between stages is essential.
Higher latency than a single call
: each stage adds a full round-trip to the LLM API. A 4-stage chain with 500ms average latency per stage adds 2 seconds of serial latency.
: naively concatenating all previous outputs into each subsequent prompt causes the context window to grow rapidly. Pass only the relevant, compacted output from each stage.
: the number of stages and their order are fixed at design time. Tasks that require dynamic branching, loops, or tool selection need a full agent loop, not a fixed chain.
: multiple API calls spend more tokens overall than a single call, even when using cheaper models for early stages. Cost analysis per chain is important.
: stage N and stage N+1 are coupled through the output schema. Changing the shape of stage-2 output requires updating stage-3's input prompt — a form of internal API versioning.
Each stage in a prompt chain is a self-contained LLM invocation. The stage has three components: a
defining the stage's role and output contract, a
containing the raw input plus any structured context accumulated from prior stages, and a
enforced either by asking the model to produce JSON and parsing it, or by using the API's native structured-output feature.

The orchestrator code runs stages in order. After each stage it validates the parsed output against the expected schema using a Pydantic model or equivalent. If validation fails, the orchestrator either retries the stage with a clarifying prompt, raises an exception, or returns an error response — depending on the stage's
. If validation passes, the output is passed forward as context to the next stage.
Context accumulation follows a pattern: rather than appending every previous system prompt and response verbatim, the orchestrator extracts and compacts the
from each stage's structured output and injects them as a tightly scoped JSON block. This keeps each stage's prompt small and focused, even after many upstream stages have run.
The total latency of a k-stage chain where each stage has mean latency
when stages are executed sequentially. Stages that do not depend on each other can be run in parallel to reduce wall-clock time to the critical path — typically the slowest generation stage.
Every stage should produce output in a well-defined schema. The two approaches are: (1)
— instruct the model to respond with a JSON object matching a specified schema, parse with

— use the provider's built-in structured-output feature (e.g. Anthropic's tool-use with a strict JSON schema) to guarantee schema conformance at the API level. Option 2 eliminates parse errors but is unavailable on all models and all providers.

The diagram below shows a four-stage customer support chain. Raw user text enters on the left. Stage 1 (Extract) uses a fast model at temperature 0 to pull structured entities and intent. Stage 2 (Classify) categorises the request into a routing label and confidence score. Stage 3 (Generate) uses a capable model with higher temperature to draft the response, conditioned on all upstream structured data. Stage 4 (Gate) is a cheap quality check that decides whether to return the draft or trigger a retry on stage 3.

attrs.alt: Sequential prompts pipeline: four-stage architecture diagram

content.text: Sequential prompt chains fit well when the task can be decomposed into a fixed, known sequence of transformation steps. Good indicators include: the sub-tasks have different quality-speed-cost trade-offs (some need a powerful model, others do not), intermediate outputs need to be logged for compliance or debugging, early stages can short-circuit and save cost, and the overall task is too complex for reliable single-prompt execution but too predictable to need a dynamic agent loop.

: document processing pipelines (OCR → extract → summarise → classify → route), customer support bots (intent detection → slot filling → policy lookup → response drafting → safety review), and content creation pipelines (outline → section drafting → tone check → SEO tagging).

: tasks where the number of steps is unknown at design time, tasks requiring tool selection and dynamic reasoning (use a ReAct loop instead), or simple tasks that a single well-crafted prompt handles reliably.
when control flow must be auditable (regulated industries), latency must be bounded (customer-facing SLAs), and the task decomposition is stable (not changing weekly).
: when the path through the task is unknown upfront, when the model needs to select and call tools dynamically, or when the task requires iterative refinement based on intermediate results.

Implementation follows five mechanical steps: define stage schemas with Pydantic, write a stage-runner helper that handles the LLM call and schema validation, compose stages into an ordered pipeline function, add a gate stage that validates the final output, and wire up retry logic and error handling at the orchestrator level.
Define a Pydantic model for each stage's output schema. Make every field typed with clear descriptions.
Write one system prompt per stage. Keep it to a single task. Include output format instructions and examples.
function that calls the LLM, parses the response, validates against the schema, and retries on validation failure.
In the orchestrator, call stages in order, passing structured context forward. Never pass raw LLM response text — always pass parsed, validated objects.
Add observability: log each stage's input context, model used, token usage, latency, and output before returning.

The example below shows a complete working chain for a customer support use case. Stage 1 extracts structured information from a raw message. Stage 2 classifies the request into a routing category. Stage 3 generates a context-aware response using the outputs of both prior stages.

"""simple_chain.py — three-stage sequential prompt chain."""
from __future__ import annotations
import json
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


# ── Stage 1 schema ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class ExtractedInfo(BaseModel):
    product_name: str
    issue_type: str   # e.g. "billing", "technical", "feature_request"
    urgency: str      # "low" | "medium" | "high"
    summary: str


# ── Stage 2 schema ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class Classification(BaseModel):
    department: str   # e.g. "billing", "tier2_support", "product"
    priority: int     # 1 (highest) – 5 (lowest)
    route_to_human: bool


# ── Helpers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def llm(system: str, user: str, model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5",
        temperature: float = 0.0) -> str:
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model=model,
        max_tokens=512,
        temperature=temperature,
        system=system,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text


# ── Stage 1: Extract ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def stage_extract(user_message: str) -> ExtractedInfo:
    system = """
You are an information-extraction engine. Given a customer message,
extract the following fields and respond ONLY with valid JSON matching
this schema:
  {"product_name": str, "issue_type": str, "urgency": str, "summary": str}
"""
    raw = llm(system, f"Customer message:\n{user_message}")
    return ExtractedInfo.model_validate_json(raw)


# ── Stage 2: Classify ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def stage_classify(extracted: ExtractedInfo) -> Classification:
    system = """
You are a support-ticket router. Given structured ticket info, decide which
department should handle it, assign a priority (1=highest, 5=lowest), and
decide if a human agent is required. Respond ONLY with valid JSON:
  {"department": str, "priority": int, "route_to_human": bool}
"""
    user = f"Ticket info:\n{extracted.model_dump_json()}"
    raw = llm(system, user)
    return Classification.model_validate_json(raw)


# ── Stage 3: Generate ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def stage_generate(
    original_message: str,
    extracted: ExtractedInfo,
    classification: Classification,
) -> str:
    system = """
You are a friendly support agent. Write a helpful reply to the customer.
Be concise, empathetic, and accurate.
"""
    context = {
        "original_message": original_message,
        "extracted": extracted.model_dump(),
        "routing": classification.model_dump(),
    }
    user = f"Context:\n{json.dumps(context, indent=2)}"
    return llm(system, user, model="claude-sonnet-4-5", temperature=0.7)


# ── Orchestrator ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def handle_support_ticket(message: str) -> dict:
    extracted = stage_extract(message)
    classification = stage_classify(extracted)
    response = stage_generate(message, extracted, classification)
    return {
        "extracted": extracted.model_dump(),
        "classification": classification.model_dump(),
        "response": response,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import pprint
    result = handle_support_ticket(
        "My invoice for Pro plan shows $199 but I was told it would be $99. "
        "This is the third time this month and I need it fixed today."
    )
    pprint.pprint(result)
Production chain with validation gate and retry

A production chain adds two critical ingredients: a gate stage that scores output quality and triggers a retry on stage 3 if the score is too low, and structured logging of every stage with token counts and latency.

"""production_chain.py — chain with gate, retry, and structured logging."""
from __future__ import annotations
import json, time, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel, ValidationError

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

MAX_RETRIES = 2


@dataclass
class StageResult:
    stage: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    latency_ms: float
    output: Any


@dataclass
class ChainTrace:
    stages: list[StageResult] = field(default_factory=list)
    aborted: bool = False
    abort_reason: str = ""

    @property
    def total_tokens(self) -> int:
        return sum(s.input_tokens + s.output_tokens for s in self.stages)

    @property
    def total_latency_ms(self) -> float:
        return sum(s.latency_ms for s in self.stages)


def run_stage(
    name: str,
    system: str,
    user: str,
    schema: type[BaseModel],
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5",
    temperature: float = 0.0,
    retries: int = MAX_RETRIES,
) -> tuple[BaseModel, StageResult]:
    """Run one chain stage with automatic retry on schema validation failure."""
    for attempt in range(retries + 1):
        t0 = time.monotonic()
        try:
            response = client.messages.create(
                model=model,
                max_tokens=1024,
                temperature=temperature,
                system=system,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
            )
        except anthropic.APIError as exc:
            if attempt == retries:
                raise
            log.warning("%s attempt %d API error: %s", name, attempt, exc)
            time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
            continue

        latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - t0) * 1000
        raw_text = response.content[0].text
        usage = response.usage

        try:
            parsed = schema.model_validate_json(raw_text)
        except (ValidationError, json.JSONDecodeError) as exc:
            if attempt == retries:
                raise ValueError(f"{name}: schema validation failed after {retries+1} attempts: {exc}") from exc
            log.warning("%s attempt %d validation error: %s", name, attempt, exc)
            user += f"\n\nPrevious attempt produced invalid JSON: {raw_text[:200]}. Fix and retry."
            continue

        result = StageResult(
            stage=name, model=model,
            input_tokens=usage.input_tokens,
            output_tokens=usage.output_tokens,
            latency_ms=latency_ms, output=parsed,
        )
        log.info("%s OK — %d+%d tokens, %.0f ms", name, usage.input_tokens, usage.output_tokens, latency_ms)
        return parsed, result

    raise RuntimeError("unreachable")


class GateVerdict(BaseModel):
    passed: bool
    score: int  # 1–10
    reason: str


def gate_stage(draft: str, original_query: str) -> GateVerdict:
    system = """
Score the draft response on quality (1–10, 7+ passes).
Check: accurate, concise, polite, addresses the query, no hallucinations.
Respond ONLY with JSON: {"passed": bool, "score": int, "reason": str}
"""
    user = f"Query: {original_query}\n\nDraft: {draft}"
    verdict, _ = run_stage("gate", system, user, GateVerdict)
    return verdict


class DraftOutput(BaseModel):
    response: str


def full_pipeline(query: str) -> tuple[str, ChainTrace]:
    trace = ChainTrace()

    # Stage 1: summarise the query
    summary_sys = "Summarise the user query in one sentence. JSON: {\"summary\": str}"

    class Summary(BaseModel):
        summary: str

    summary, r1 = run_stage("summarise", summary_sys, query, Summary)
    trace.stages.append(r1)

    # Stage 2: generate response
    gen_sys = "You are a helpful assistant. Write a clear, accurate answer."
    gen_user = f"Query: {query}\nContext: {summary.summary}\nJSON: {{\"response\": str}}"

    for attempt in range(MAX_RETRIES + 1):
        draft_obj, r2 = run_stage(
            f"generate-attempt-{attempt}", gen_sys, gen_user, DraftOutput,
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5", temperature=0.7,
        )
        trace.stages.append(r2)

        verdict = gate_stage(draft_obj.response, query)
        if verdict.passed:
            log.info("Gate PASSED score=%d", verdict.score)
            return draft_obj.response, trace

        log.warning("Gate FAILED score=%d reason=%s", verdict.score, verdict.reason)
        gen_user += f"\n\nPrevious draft scored {verdict.score}/10. Reason: {verdict.reason}. Improve it."

    trace.aborted = True
    trace.abort_reason = "gate failed after all retries"
    return draft_obj.response, trace  # return best attempt


if __name__ == "__main__":
    answer, trace = full_pipeline("Explain the difference between a mutex and a semaphore.")
    print(answer)
    print(f"\nTotal tokens: {trace.total_tokens} | Latency: {trace.total_latency_ms:.0f} ms")

A legal document review system processes 10,000 contracts per day using a five-stage chain. Stage 1 (Haiku, T=0) OCRs and normalises the raw document text into structured JSON with clause inventory. Stage 2 (Haiku, T=0) classifies the contract type (NDA, MSA, SaaS, employment) and extracts key dates. Stage 3 (Sonnet, T=0.1) flags non-standard clauses against a corporate playbook — comparing against 50+ known clause patterns. Stage 4 (Haiku, T=0) checks whether any flagged clause is in the predefined "must escalate to legal" list and short-circuits the pipeline with an escalation ticket if so. Stage 5 (Sonnet, T=0.3) generates a reviewer briefing summarising risk level, recommended changes, and approval path.

The chain processes 95% of contracts end-to-end in under 8 seconds. Only the 5% that hit stage 4's escalation path require immediate human review. Total token cost per contract is roughly 8,000 tokens at an average cost well below $0.02. Intermediate outputs from all stages are stored in a document database for compliance purposes — a key requirement that a black-box single-prompt approach could not satisfy.

"""async_chain.py — run independent pipeline stages concurrently."""
import asyncio
import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()


class ContractMeta(BaseModel):
    contract_type: str
    effective_date: str
    parties: list[str]


class ClauseInventory(BaseModel):
    clauses: list[str]  # e.g. ["indemnification", "limitation_of_liability"]
    page_count: int


async def extract_metadata(text: str) -> ContractMeta:
    """Stage A — extract contract metadata (runs in parallel with B)."""
    system = "Extract contract metadata. JSON: {contract_type, effective_date, parties: []}"
    msg = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=256, temperature=0.0,
        system=system, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text[:4000]}],
    )
    return ContractMeta.model_validate_json(msg.content[0].text)


async def inventory_clauses(text: str) -> ClauseInventory:
    """Stage B — enumerate clause types (runs in parallel with A)."""
    system = "List clause types present. JSON: {clauses: [], page_count: int}"
    msg = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=256, temperature=0.0,
        system=system, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": text[:4000]}],
    )
    return ClauseInventory.model_validate_json(msg.content[0].text)


async def generate_briefing(meta: ContractMeta, inventory: ClauseInventory) -> str:
    """Stage C — briefing runs only after A and B both finish."""
    system = "Write a one-paragraph contract review briefing for a lawyer."
    user = f"Metadata: {meta.model_dump_json()}\nClauses: {inventory.model_dump_json()}"
    msg = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5", max_tokens=512, temperature=0.3,
        system=system, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
    )
    return msg.content[0].text


async def review_contract(text: str) -> str:
    # Run A and B in parallel, then C sequentially
    meta, inventory = await asyncio.gather(
        extract_metadata(text),
        inventory_clauses(text),
    )
    return await generate_briefing(meta, inventory)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sample_text = "This MASTER SERVICE AGREEMENT is entered into on 2025-01-01 ..."
    briefing = asyncio.run(review_contract(sample_text))
    print(briefing)

: piping the raw LLM response string from stage N directly into stage N+1's prompt without parsing and validating means schema errors propagate invisibly. Always parse → validate → pass structured objects.
Accumulating full context at every stage
: appending the full system prompt and response of every prior stage to the next stage's prompt causes context to grow quadratically. Extract only the fields the next stage needs.
Using the same model and temperature for every stage
: extraction and classification stages should use temperature 0 and a fast model. Using a powerful model at high temperature for deterministic tasks wastes money and introduces non-determinism.
No retry logic on schema validation failure
: models occasionally produce malformed JSON even with careful prompting. Without retry logic, a single bad output kills the entire chain. Add at least one retry with the error message appended.
Skipping the gate stage in production
: without a gate, low-quality outputs from stage 3 reach end users. A cheap Haiku gate call costs a fraction of the generation call and prevents bad outputs from escaping.
: a 10-stage chain with micro-steps each doing trivial work adds latency and complexity. Merge stages that are always called together and have the same model requirements.
: start with the data contract between stages. Every field should have a clear type and docstring. This drives the prompt wording and makes integration tests straightforward.
Use a single generic run_stage() helper
: centralise retry logic, logging, and token accounting in one function. Stage-specific code is only the system prompt and schema — not the retry/parse loop.
Choose model and temperature per stage explicitly
: extraction and classification → cheap model, temperature 0. Generation → powerful model, temperature 0.3–0.7. Gate → cheap model, temperature 0.
Log stage traces to a database
: storing every stage's input, output, token usage, latency, and model creates an audit trail and enables offline evaluation against golden sets.
: if stages A and B do not depend on each other's output, use
to run them concurrently and reduce wall-clock latency.
: extraction and classification stages with temperature 0 are deterministic for the same input. Cache their outputs with a content-hash key to save tokens on re-runs of the same document.
Write evals per stage, not just end-to-end
: maintain a golden test set for each stage. Stage-level evals catch prompt regressions earlier and localise failures more precisely than end-to-end tests.

The total latency of a k-stage serial chain is dominated by the slowest stage — usually the generation stage on a large model. For a system with a 2-second P50 latency requirement, a 4-stage chain needs each stage to average under 500ms. Fast classification models (Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) deliver 200–400ms for short prompts, leaving the budget for a single 600–800ms generation call.
: stages that do not depend on each other can run concurrently (asyncio / threading). This converts serial latency into parallel latency for the critical path.

: Anthropic's prompt caching caches the system prompt token prefix, reducing time-to-first-token on repeated calls with the same system prompt. This is especially effective for extraction stages with long, stable system prompts.
Result caching at the stage level
: for deterministic stages (temperature 0), cache the output keyed by a hash of the input. Subsequent runs with the same input skip the LLM call entirely.
: a fast cheap gate at stage 2 that rejects out-of-scope inputs before reaching the expensive generation stage saves cost and latency proportional to the out-of-scope traffic fraction.
: for user-facing chains, stream the final generation stage's response to the client while it is being produced. This reduces perceived latency significantly for long outputs.

Prompt chains can be built directly with the Anthropic SDK or any LLM provider SDK without any framework. This keeps dependencies minimal and makes the pipeline fully transparent. For teams that want higher-level abstractions, LangChain's
(LangChain Expression Language) and LlamaIndex's pipeline primitives provide chain DSLs with built-in tracing, but they add significant abstraction overhead and make debugging harder.
: direct API access, full control. Use
for safe JSON parsing with type coercion and clear validation errors.
: observability platforms that capture stage-level traces, token counts, latencies, and evaluation scores. Highly recommended for production chains.
: browser-based environment for iterating on individual stage prompts without running the full chain — useful for rapid prompt development.
: workflow orchestration platforms suitable for long-running chains with persistence, retries, and fan-out across many documents.
The table below compares prompt chaining against the most common alternative approaches for multi-step LLM tasks.
Prompt chains introduce unique security and reliability concerns because each stage can be independently attacked and errors compound across stages.

: if user-controlled content passes through stage 1 and is injected verbatim into stage 2's prompt, an attacker can embed instructions that hijack stage 2's behaviour. Always wrap user-sourced content in a delimited block and instruct the model to treat it as data, not instructions.

: an attacker who can influence stage 1's input may be able to produce structured output that causes stage 2 to behave unexpectedly. Validate all inter-stage outputs against strict schemas with maximum field lengths.
: set a per-stage timeout. If any stage exceeds the timeout budget, abort the chain and return a graceful fallback. Never let a stalled API call block the chain indefinitely.
per stage appropriate to the expected output size. A runaway generation stage that produces 10,000 tokens bloats downstream context and costs unexpectedly.

: if the input contains personal data, apply a redact-then-process-then-reinstate pattern. Run stage 1 to replace PII with placeholders, run the chain on anonymised data, then substitute PII back into the final output.
: the gate stage is also the place to run content moderation. Check the final generated output for policy violations before returning it to the caller.

Think of a prompt chain as an assembly line in a factory. Each worker on the line is a specialist who receives a half-finished product from the previous worker, performs one specific operation, and passes their output to the next. No worker tries to do everything. The factory manager (orchestrator code) controls the conveyor belt — they decide the sequence, check quality at each station, and can halt the line if a defect is detected. The workers (LLM calls) are not allowed to deviate from their station or communicate directly with each other — everything goes through the belt as structured material.
A single LLM trying to do ten things at once is like a lone craftsperson building a car. A prompt chain is an assembly line — slower per station, but massively better at quality, cost, and scale.

A sequential prompt chain is a fixed pipeline of LLM calls where each stage receives the previous stage's validated structured output as context — control flow lives in the calling code, not the model.
between stages, never raw text strings. Pydantic schemas are the contract between stages.
Use the cheapest, fastest model appropriate for each stage: temperature 0 and Haiku-class for deterministic extraction/classification, Sonnet-class for nuanced generation.
A gate stage — a cheap quality-verification call after the generation stage — is the most impactful addition to any production chain, preventing low-quality output from reaching users.
Log every stage's inputs, outputs, token counts, and latency. Per-stage observability is what makes chains superior to single prompts for production debugging.
Independent stages can be parallelised with
to reduce wall-clock latency from serial sum to critical-path length.
. For tasks where the number of steps or tools to use is unknown at design time, use a dynamic agent loop instead.
Guard against prompt injection by always wrapping user-controlled content as delimited data in inter-stage prompts, and validate all inter-stage output fields against maximum-length constraints.
